lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Here pitch our tents, even here in Bosworth field.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: My Lord of Surrey, why look you so sad?

lines.speaker: SURREY
lines.text: My heart is ten times lighter than my looks.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: My Lord of Norfolk—

lines.speaker: NORFOLK
lines.text: Here, most gracious liege.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Norfolk, we must have knocks; ha! must we not?

lines.speaker: NORFOLK
lines.text: We must both give and take, my gracious lord.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Up with my tent there! here will I lie to-night;

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: But where to-morrow? Well, all’s one for that.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Who hath descried the number of the foe?

lines.speaker: NORFOLK
lines.text: Six or seven thousand is their utmost power.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Why, our battalion trebles that account:

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Besides, the king’s name is a tower of strength,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Which they upon the adverse party want.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Up with my tent there! Valiant gentlemen,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Let us survey the vantage of the field;

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Call for some men of sound direction:

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Let’s want no discipline, make no delay;

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: For, lords, to-morrow is a busy day.

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: The weary sun hath made a golden set,

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: And by the bright track of his fiery car,

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: Gives signal of a goodly day to-morrow.

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: Sir William Brandon, you shall bear my standard.

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: Give me some ink and paper in my tent:

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: I’ll draw the form and model of our battle,

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: Limit each leader to his several charge,

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: And part in just proportion our small strength.

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: My Lord of Oxford, you, Sir William Brandon,

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: And you, Sir Walter Herbert, stay with me.

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: The Earl of Pembroke keeps his regiment:

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: Good Captain Blunt, bear my good-night to him,

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: And by the second hour in the morning

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: Desire the earl to see me in my tent:

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: Yet one thing more, good Blunt, before thou go’st,

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: Where is Lord Stanley quarter’d, dost thou know?

lines.speaker: BLUNT
lines.text: Unless I have mista’en his colours much,

lines.speaker: BLUNT
lines.text: Which well I am assured I have not done,

lines.speaker: BLUNT
lines.text: His regiment lies half a mile at least

lines.speaker: BLUNT
lines.text: South from the mighty power of the king.

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: If without peril it be possible,

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: Good Captain Blunt, bear my good-night to him,

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: And give him from me this most needful scroll.

lines.speaker: BLUNT
lines.text: Upon my life, my lord, I’ll undertake it;

lines.speaker: BLUNT
lines.text: And so, God give you quiet rest to-night!

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: Good night, good Captain Blunt. Come gentlemen,

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: Let us consult upon to-morrow’s business:

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: In to our tent; the air is raw and cold. They withdraw into the tent.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: What is’t o’clock?

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: It’s supper-time, my lord;

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: It’s nine o’clock.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: I will not sup to-night.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Give me some ink and paper.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: What, is my beaver easier than it was?

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: And all my armour laid into my tent?

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: It is, my liege; and all things are in readiness.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Good Norfolk, hie thee to thy charge;

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Use careful watch, choose trusty sentinels.

lines.speaker: NORFOLK
lines.text: I go, my lord.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Stir with the lark to-morrow, gentle Norfolk.

lines.speaker: NORFOLK
lines.text: I warrant you, my lord.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Catesby!

lines.speaker: CATESBY
lines.text: My lord?

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Send out a pursuivant at arms

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: To Stanley’s regiment; bid him bring his power

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Before sunrising, lest his son George fall

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Into the blind cave of eternal night.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Fill me a bowl of wine. Give me a watch.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Saddle white Surrey for the field to-morrow.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Look that my staves be sound, and not too heavy.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Ratcliff!

lines.speaker: RATCLIFF
lines.text: My lord?

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Saw’st thou the melancholy Lord Northumberland?

lines.speaker: RATCLIFF
lines.text: Thomas the Earl of Surrey, and himself,

lines.speaker: RATCLIFF
lines.text: Much about cock-shut time, from troop to troop

lines.speaker: RATCLIFF
lines.text: Went through the army, cheering up the soldiers.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: So, I am satisfied. Give me a bowl of wine:

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: I have not that alacrity of spirit,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Nor cheer of mind, that I was wont to have.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Set it down. Is ink and paper ready?

lines.speaker: RATCLIFF
lines.text: It is, my lord.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Bid my guard watch; leave me.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Ratcliff, about the mid of night come to my tent

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: And help to arm me. Leave me, I say.

lines.speaker: DERBY
lines.text: Fortune and victory sit on thy helm!

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: All comfort that the dark night can afford

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: Be to thy person, noble father-in-law!

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: Tell me, how fares our loving mother?

lines.speaker: DERBY
lines.text: I, by attorney, bless thee from thy mother,

lines.speaker: DERBY
lines.text: Who prays continually for Richmond’s good:

lines.speaker: DERBY
lines.text: So much for that. The silent hours steal on,

lines.speaker: DERBY
lines.text: And flaky darkness breaks within the east.

lines.speaker: DERBY
lines.text: In brief—for so the season bids us be—

lines.speaker: DERBY
lines.text: Prepare thy battle early in the morning,

lines.speaker: DERBY
lines.text: And put thy fortune to the arbitrement

lines.speaker: DERBY
lines.text: Of bloody strokes and mortal-staring war.

lines.speaker: DERBY
lines.text: I, as I may—that which I would I cannot—

lines.speaker: DERBY
lines.text: With best advantage will deceive the time,

lines.speaker: DERBY
lines.text: And aid thee in this doubtful shock of arms:

lines.speaker: DERBY
lines.text: But on thy side I may not be too forward,

lines.speaker: DERBY
lines.text: Lest, being seen, thy brother, tender George,

lines.speaker: DERBY
lines.text: Be executed in his father’s sight.

lines.speaker: DERBY
lines.text: Farewell: the leisure and the fearful time

lines.speaker: DERBY
lines.text: Cuts off the ceremonious vows of love

lines.speaker: DERBY
lines.text: And ample interchange of sweet discourse,

lines.speaker: DERBY
lines.text: Which so long sunder’d friends should dwell upon:

lines.speaker: DERBY
lines.text: God give us leisure for these rites of love!

lines.speaker: DERBY
lines.text: Once more, adieu: be valiant, and speed well!

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: Good lords, conduct him to his regiment:

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: I’ll strive, with troubled thoughts, to take a nap,

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: Lest leaden slumber peise me down to-morrow,

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: When I should mount with wings of victory:

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: Once more, good night, kind lords and gentlemen.

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: O Thou, whose captain I account myself,

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: Look on my forces with a gracious eye;

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: Put in their hands thy bruising irons of wrath,

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: That they may crush down with a heavy fall

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: The usurping helmets of our adversaries!

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: Make us thy ministers of chastisement,

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: That we may praise thee in the victory!

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: To thee I do commend my watchful soul,

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: Ere I let fall the windows of mine eyes:

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: Sleeping and waking, O, defend me still! Sleeps.

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: To Richard. Let me sit heavy on thy soul to-morrow!

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Think, how thou stab’dst me in my prime of youth

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: At Tewksbury: despair, therefore, and die!

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: To Richmond. Be cheerful, Richmond; for the wronged souls

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Of butcher’d princes fight in thy behalf:

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: King Henry’s issue, Richmond, comforts thee.

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: To Richard. When I was mortal, my anointed body

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: By thee was punched full of deadly holes:

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Think on the Tower and me: despair, and die!

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Harry the Sixth bids thee despair and die!

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: To Richmond. Virtuous and holy, be thou conqueror!

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Harry, that prophesied thou shouldst be king,

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Doth comfort thee in thy sleep: live, and flourish!

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: To Richard. Let me sit heavy on thy soul to-morrow!

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: I, that was wash’d to death with fulsome wine,

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Poor Clarence, by thy guile betrayed to death!

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: To-morrow in the battle think on me,

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: And fall thy edgeless sword: despair, and die!—

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: To Richmond. Thou offspring of the house of Lancaster,

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: The wronged heirs of York do pray for thee:

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Good angels guard thy battle! live, and flourish!

lines.speaker: GHOST OF RIVERS
lines.text: To Richard. Let me sit heavy on thy soul to-morrow,

lines.speaker: GHOST OF RIVERS
lines.text: Rivers, that died at Pomfret! despair, and die!

lines.speaker: GHOST OF GREY
lines.text: To Richard. Think upon Grey, and let thy soul despair!

lines.speaker: GHOST OF VAUGHAN
lines.text: To Richard. Think upon Vaughan, and, with guilty fear,

lines.speaker: GHOST OF VAUGHAN
lines.text: Let fall thy lance: despair, and die!

lines.speaker: ALL
lines.text: To Richmond. Awake, and think our wrongs in Richard’s bosom

lines.speaker: ALL
lines.text: Will conquer him! awake, and win the day!

lines.speaker: GHOST OF HASTINGS
lines.text: To Richard. Bloody and guilty, guiltily awake,

lines.speaker: GHOST OF HASTINGS
lines.text: And in a bloody battle end thy days!

lines.speaker: GHOST OF HASTINGS
lines.text: Think on Lord Hastings: despair, and die!

lines.speaker: GHOST OF HASTINGS
lines.text: To Richmond. Quiet untroubled soul, awake, awake!

lines.speaker: GHOST OF HASTINGS
lines.text: Arm, fight, and conquer, for fair England’s sake!

lines.speaker: GHOSTS
lines.text: To Richard. Dream on thy cousins smother’d in the Tower:

lines.speaker: GHOSTS
lines.text: Let us be lead within thy bosom, Richard,

lines.speaker: GHOSTS
lines.text: And weigh thee down to ruin, shame, and death!

lines.speaker: GHOSTS
lines.text: Thy nephews’ souls bid thee despair and die!

lines.speaker: GHOSTS
lines.text: To Richmond. Sleep, Richmond, sleep in peace, and wake in joy;

lines.speaker: GHOSTS
lines.text: Good angels guard thee from the boar’s annoy!

lines.speaker: GHOSTS
lines.text: Live, and beget a happy race of kings!

lines.speaker: GHOSTS
lines.text: Edward’s unhappy sons do bid thee flourish.

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: To Richard. Richard, thy wife, that wretched Anne thy wife,

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: That never slept a quiet hour with thee,

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Now fills thy sleep with perturbations:

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: To-morrow in the battle think on me,

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: And fall thy edgeless sword: despair, and die!

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: To Richmond. Thou quiet soul, sleep thou a quiet sleep;

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Dream of success and happy victory!

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Thy adversary’s wife doth pray for thee.

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: To Richard. The last was I that help’d thee to the crown;

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: The last was I that felt thy tyranny:

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: O, in the battle think on Buckingham,

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: And die in terror of thy guiltiness!

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Dream on, dream on, of bloody deeds and death:

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: Fainting, despair; despairing, yield thy breath!

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: To Richmond. I died for hope ere I could lend thee aid:

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: But cheer thy heart, and be thou not dismay’d:

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: God and good angel fight on Richmond’s side;

lines.speaker: GHOST
lines.text: And Richard falls in height of all his pride. The Ghosts vanish. King Richard starts out of his dream.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Give me another horse: bind up my wounds.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Have mercy, Jesu!—Soft! I did but dream.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: O coward conscience, how dost thou afflict me!

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: The lights burn blue. It is now dead midnight.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Cold fearful drops stand on my trembling flesh.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: What do I fear? myself? there’s none else by:

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Richard loves Richard; that is, I am I.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Is there a murderer here? No. Yes, I am:

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Then fly. What, from myself? Great reason why:

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Lest I revenge. What, myself upon myself?

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Alack. I love myself. Wherefore? for any good

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: That I myself have done unto myself?

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: O, no! alas, I rather hate myself

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: For hateful deeds committed by myself!

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: I am a villain: yet I lie, I am not.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Fool, of thyself speak well: fool, do not flatter.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: My conscience hath a thousand several tongues,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: And every tongue brings in a several tale,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: And every tale condemns me for a villain.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Perjury, perjury, in the high’st degree;

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Murder, stern murder, in the direst degree;

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: All several sins, all used in each degree,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Throng to the bar, crying all, Guilty! guilty!

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: I shall despair. There is no creature loves me;

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: And if I die, no soul shall pity me:

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Nay, wherefore should they, since that I myself

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Find in myself no pity to myself?

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Methought the souls of all that I had murder’d

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Came to my tent; and every one did threat

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: To-morrow’s vengeance on the head of Richard.

lines.speaker: RATCLIFF
lines.text: My lord!

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: ’Zounds! who is there?

lines.speaker: RATCLIFF
lines.text: Ratcliff, my lord; ’tis I. The early village-cock

lines.speaker: RATCLIFF
lines.text: Hath twice done salutation to the morn;

lines.speaker: RATCLIFF
lines.text: Your friends are up, and buckle on their armour.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: O Ratcliff, I have dream’d a fearful dream!

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: What thinkest thou, will our friends prove all true?

lines.speaker: RATCLIFF
lines.text: No doubt, my lord.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: O Ratcliff, I fear, I fear—

lines.speaker: RATCLIFF
lines.text: Nay, good my lord, be not afraid of shadows.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: By the apostle Paul, shadows to-night

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Have struck more terror to the soul of Richard

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Than can the substance of ten thousand soldiers

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Armed in proof, and led by shallow Richmond.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: It is not yet near day. Come, go with me;

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Under our tents I’ll play the eaves-dropper,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: To see if any mean to shrink from me.

lines.speaker: LORDS
lines.text: Good morrow, Richmond!

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: Cry mercy, lords and watchful gentlemen,

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: That you have ta’en a tardy sluggard here.

lines.speaker: LORDS
lines.text: How have you slept, my lord?

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: The sweetest sleep, and fairest-boding dreams

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: That ever enter’d in a drowsy head,

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: Have I since your departure had, my lords.

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: Methought their souls, whose bodies Richard murder’d,

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: Came to my tent, and cried on victory:

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: I promise you, my soul is very jocund

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: In the remembrance of so fair a dream.

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: How far into the morning is it, lords?

lines.speaker: LORDS
lines.text: Upon the stroke of four.

lines.speaker: RICHMOND
lines.text: Why, then ’tis time to arm and give direction.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: What said Northumberland as touching Richmond?

lines.speaker: RATCLIFF
lines.text: That he was never trained up in arms.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: He said the truth: and what said Surrey then?

lines.speaker: RATCLIFF
lines.text: He smiled and said “The better for our purpose.”

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: He was in the right; and so indeed it is. Clock striketh.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Ten the clock there. Give me a calendar.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Who saw the sun to-day?

lines.speaker: RATCLIFF
lines.text: Not I, my lord.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Then he disdains to shine; for by the book

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: He should have braved the east an hour ago:

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: A black day will it be to somebody.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Ratcliff!

lines.speaker: RATCLIFF
lines.text: My lord?

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: The sun will not be seen to-day;

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: The sky doth frown and lour upon our army.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: I would these dewy tears were from the ground.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Not shine to-day! Why, what is that to me

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: More than to Richmond? for the selfsame heaven

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: That frowns on me looks sadly upon him.

lines.speaker: NORFOLK
lines.text: Arm, arm, my lord; the foe vaunts in the field.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Come, bustle, bustle; caparison my horse.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Call up Lord Stanley, bid him bring his power:

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: I will lead forth my soldiers to the plain,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: And thus my battle shall be ordered:

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: My foreward shall be drawn out all in length,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Consisting equally of horse and foot;

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Our archers shall be placed in the midst:

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: John Duke of Norfolk, Thomas Earl of Surrey,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Shall have the leading of this foot and horse.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: They thus directed, we will follow

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: In the main battle, whose puissance on either side

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Shall be well winged with our chiefest horse.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: This, and Saint George to boot! What think’st thou, Norfolk?

lines.speaker: NORFOLK
lines.text: A good direction, warlike sovereign.

lines.speaker: NORFOLK
lines.text: This found I on my tent this morning. He showeth him a paper.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: “Jockey of Norfolk, be not too bold,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: For Dickon thy master is bought and sold.”

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: A thing devised by the enemy.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Go, gentleman, every man unto his charge:

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Let not our babbling dreams affright our souls:

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Conscience is but a word that cowards use,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Devised at first to keep the strong in awe:

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Our strong arms be our conscience, swords our law.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: March on, join bravely, let us to’t pell-mell;

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: If not to heaven, then hand in hand to hell.

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: My lord, he doth deny to come.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Off with his son George’s head!

lines.speaker: NORFOLK
lines.text: My lord, the enemy is past the marsh:

lines.speaker: NORFOLK
lines.text: After the battle let George Stanley die.

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: A thousand hearts are great within my bosom:

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Advance our standards, set upon our foes;

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Our ancient word of courage, fair Saint George,

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Inspire us with the spleen of fiery dragons!

lines.speaker: KING RICHARD
lines.text: Upon them! victory sits on our helms.

stage_directions.text: Enter King Richard in arms, with Norfolk, the Earl of Surrey, and others.
Exeunt.
Enter, on the other side of the field, Richmond, Sir William Brandon, Oxford, and others. Some of the Soldiers pitch Richmond’s tent.
Enter, to his tent, King Richard, Norfolk, Ratcliff, Catesby, and others.
Exit.
Exit Catesby.
Exeunt Ratcliff and the other Attendants.
Enter Derby to Richmond in his tent, Lords and others attending.
Exeunt all but Richmond.
Enter the Ghost of Prince Edward, son to Henry the Sixth.
Enter the Ghost of Henry the Sixth.
Enter the Ghost of Clarence.
Enter the Ghosts of Rivers, Grey, and Vaughan.
Enter the Ghost of Hastings.
Enter the Ghosts of the two young Princes.
Enter the Ghost of Lady Anne.
Enter the Ghost of Buckingham.
Enter Ratcliff.
Exeunt.
Enter the Lords to Richmond, sitting in his tent.
His oration to his soldiers.

More than I have said, loving countrymen, The leisure and enforcement of the time Forbids to dwell upon: yet remember this, God and our good cause fight upon our side; The prayers of holy saints and wronged souls, Like high-rear’d bulwarks, stand before our faces; Richard except, those whom we fight against Had rather have us win than him they follow: For what is he they follow? truly, gentlemen, A bloody tyrant and a homicide; One raised in blood, and one in blood establish’d; One that made means to come by what he hath, And slaughter’d those that were the means to help him; A base foul stone, made precious by the foil Of England’s chair, where he is falsely set; One that hath ever been God’s enemy: Then, if you fight against God’s enemy, God will in justice ward you as his soldiers; If you do sweat to put a tyrant down, You sleep in peace, the tyrant being slain; If you do fight against your country’s foes, Your country’s fat shall pay your pains the hire; If you do fight in safeguard of your wives, Your wives shall welcome home the conquerors; If you do free your children from the sword, Your children’s children quit it in your age. Then, in the name of God and all these rights, Advance your standards, draw your willing swords. For me, the ransom of my bold attempt Shall be this cold corpse on the earth’s cold face; But if I thrive, the gain of my attempt The least of you shall share his part thereof. Sound drums and trumpets boldly and cheerfully; God and Saint George! Richmond and victory! Exeunt.
Reenter King Richard, Ratcliff, Attendants and Forces.
Enter Norfolk.
Reads.
His oration to his Army.

What shall I say more than I have inferr’d? Remember whom you are to cope withal; A sort of vagabonds, rascals, and runaways, A scum of Bretons, and base lackey peasants, Whom their o’er-cloyed country vomits forth To desperate ventures and assured destruction. You sleeping safe, they bring to you unrest; You having lands, and blest with beauteous wives, They would restrain the one, distain the other. And who doth lead them but a paltry fellow, Long kept in Bretagne at our mother’s cost? A milk-sop, one that never in his life Felt so much cold as over shoes in snow? Let’s whip these stragglers o’er the seas again; Lash hence these overweening rags of France, These famish’d beggars, weary of their lives; Who, but for dreaming on this fond exploit, For want of means, poor rats, had hang’d themselves: If we be conquer’d, let men conquer us, And not these bastard Bretons; whom our fathers Have in their own land beaten, bobb’d, and thump’d, And in record, left them the heirs of shame. Shall these enjoy our lands? lie with our wives? Ravish our daughters? Drum afar off. Hark! I hear their drum. Fight, gentlemen of England! fight, bold yoemen! Draw, archers, draw your arrows to the head! Spur your proud horses hard, and ride in blood; Amaze the welkin with your broken staves!
Enter a Messenger.
What says Lord Stanley? will he bring his power?
Exeunt.

glosses.definition: Bosworth Field, Leicestershire — 22 August 1485; last battle of the Wars of the Roses. Richard's death site
must trade blows — Richard's blunt soldier-talk
our force is three times theirs — historically Richard had ~10,000 to Richmond's ~5,000

glosses.phrase: king’s name is a tower of strength
glosses.definition: the very title of king gives might — proverbial; cf. Proverbs 18:10
men of reliable judgement

glosses.phrase: weary sun hath made a golden set
glosses.definition: the tired sun has set in gold — Richmond's portent-reading, favourable
the sun as a fiery chariot — classical image (Apollo/Phaethon)

glosses.phrase: Sir William Brandon, you shall bear my standard
glosses.definition: Brandon was Richmond's standard-bearer — historically killed by Richard in single combat on the field

glosses.phrase: Limit each leader to his several charge
glosses.definition: assign each commander his specific duty
military banners — identifying a regiment
the movable front-piece of a helmet
rise at dawn — proverbial
the grave — death as a dark cave
Richard's warhorse, named for its pale colouring and origin (the county of Surrey)
spears or lances
dusk; the time when poultry are shut up — a rural time-marker
briskness, quickness of mind — Richard notes his own dwindling

glosses.phrase: by attorney, bless thee from thy mother
glosses.definition: by proxy, give you your mother's blessing — Margaret Beaufort in absentia

glosses.phrase: flaky darkness breaks within the east
glosses.definition: dawn breaks in flakes (the first grey streaks) in the east
arbitration, decision — the battle as formal legal judgement
war that stares with deadly eyes
heavy sleep weigh me down — 'peise' = weigh
bruising weapons of God's wrath — Richmond asks God to arm his soldiers
agents of punishment
eyelids — the 'windows' through which the soul looks out (cf. 1.2.12)

glosses.phrase: Let me sit heavy on thy soul
glosses.definition: let me weigh on your conscience — the refrain each ghost delivers, with variations
the 1471 battle where Richard killed Prince Edward of Lancaster — the play's recurrent crime-reference
each ghost's theological curse — despair (the sin against the Holy Spirit) = damnation without repentance

glosses.phrase: Think on the Tower and me
glosses.definition: Henry VI's murder at the Tower, 1471 — dramatized in 3 Henry VI 5.6

glosses.phrase: wash’d to death with fulsome wine
glosses.definition: drowned in cloying wine — Clarence's malmsey-butt death (1.4.135)
Rivers names his own death-site (cf. 3.3)
the two young princes — cousins (kin) to Richmond through both York and Lancaster lines

glosses.phrase: Let us be lead within thy bosom
glosses.definition: let us be lead within your breast — leaden weight, not lead as in metal direction
the boar's harm — 'annoy' = injury
troublings, disturbances

glosses.phrase: Fainting, despair; despairing, yield thy breath
glosses.definition: as you faint, despair; as you despair, die — Buckingham's curse-chain

glosses.phrase: died for hope ere I could lend thee aid
glosses.definition: Buckingham died hoping to aid Richmond, before he could act
Richard waking from the ghost-dream — his first disoriented command anticipates 5.4
hold; wait — an interjection meaning 'stop and think'
candles burn blue — the folk-sign of ghostly presence or death

glosses.phrase: Richard loves Richard; that is, I am I
glosses.definition: the speech's famous solipsistic tautology — Richard's selfhood disintegrating into empty equation

glosses.phrase: I am a villain: yet I lie, I am not
glosses.definition: Richard's logical impasse: he is a villain AND he denies it — the self-division the ghosts have produced

glosses.phrase: conscience hath a thousand several tongues
glosses.definition: my conscience has a thousand distinct tongues — each a separate accusation. The clearest moment of interiority the play grants Richard
crowd to the court-bar — Richard's sins as plaintiffs
theological despair (cf. 1.2.84; the ghosts' curse achieved) — Richard recognizes his damnation
strap on their armor for battle
afraid of unreal things — Ratcliff's dismissal
armed in tested (proven) armor
one who listens under the eaves of a house — here Richard as spy on his own camp
cried out for victory (to be granted to Richmond)
cheerful, merry
the enemy boasts on the battlefield — 'vaunts' = boasts loudly
put the caparison (ornamental cloth and armor) on my horse
vanguard; front division of an army
military might; force
St George (England's patron saint) in addition — war-cry
the mocking placard mounted on Norfolk's tent — 'Jockey' = familiar form of 'John', i.e. Duke John of Norfolk

glosses.phrase: Dickon thy master is bought and sold
glosses.definition: 'Dickon' = familiar for 'Richard'; the couplet is historical, cited by Holinshed

glosses.phrase: Conscience is but a word that cowards use
glosses.definition: Richard's notorious dismissal of conscience — his public counter to the 'thousand tongues' soliloquy's private recognition
in confused, headlong fashion

glosses.phrase: If not to heaven, then hand in hand to hell
glosses.definition: Richard's battle-motto — the most morally naked line in the play
fierce passion of dragons — the spleen was the seat of fury

annotations.title: The ghost procession: Shakespeare's most extensive haunting
annotations.body: Ten ghosts appear to Richard in turn — Prince Edward of Lancaster, Henry VI, Clarence, Rivers, Grey, Vaughan, Hastings, the two young Princes (collectively), Lady Anne, and Buckingham — in the order of their deaths within the play's chronology. This is the most extensive ghost sequence in Shakespeare; the nearest comparisons are Banquo's single appearance in Macbeth 3.4 and the Caesar-visitation in Julius Caesar 4.3. The ghost scene's basic form is strictly formal: each ghost addresses Richard first ("despair, and die!") and then turns to Richmond, sleeping in his own tent at the other side of the stage, to bless him ("live, and flourish!"). The symmetry is the structure. Every ghost curses Richard in the vocabulary Margaret began ("despair"), blesses Richmond in the vocabulary the Duchess's maternal prayer has set up, and exits. The curse-chain Margaret started in 1.3, the maternal prayer the Duchess added in 4.4, and the souls of the murdered converge here as a single ledger-settlement.
sources: Wolfgang Clemen, A Commentary on Shakespeare's Richard III (1968)
Marjorie Garber, Shakespeare's Ghost Writers (1987)
Stephen Greenblatt, Hamlet in Purgatory (2001)

annotations.title: The moral-accounting function of the ghosts
annotations.body: Each ghost names a specific murder. Prince Edward cites Tewkesbury. Henry VI cites his own murder at the Tower (1471). Clarence cites the malmsey-butt drowning. Rivers, Grey, and Vaughan cite Pomfret. Hastings cites the council-chamber trap. The Young Princes cite the smothering in the Tower. Anne cites her own poisoned marriage-bed. Buckingham cites his betrayal at Salisbury. The sequence is the play's ledger, recited in order, one death at a time. The ghosts are not merely frightening — they are a courtroom. Each arrives to testify specifically. Richard's "My conscience hath a thousand several tongues, / And every tongue brings in a several tale, / And every tale condemns me for a villain" (190–192) names what the ghost sequence has just enacted: his conscience is not a single voice but a courtroom of testimonies, each specific, each separate. Shakespeare has given us in stage form what Richard will immediately re-describe in his own words.
sources: Wolfgang Clemen, A Commentary on Shakespeare's Richard III (1968)
Harold Bloom, Shakespeare: The Invention of the Human (1998)
Maus, Katharine Eisaman, Inwardness and Theater in the English Renaissance (1995)

annotations.title: "I am I" — the soliloquy of self-division
annotations.body: Richard's waking soliloquy is the most philosophically interesting speech in the play. "Richard loves Richard; that is, I am I" is a formal tautology — the self equaling the self — but it fails as a consolation. He immediately multiplies: "Is there a murderer here? No. Yes, I am: / Then fly. What, from myself?" The speech stages a self that cannot hold together. The pronouns themselves unravel: myself, I myself, I, I. "I am a villain: yet I lie, I am not." He cannot both condemn and absolve himself in consistent voice. This is the interiority the play has been pointing toward since 1.1 and has so far only given us in inverted form (the Second Murderer in 1.4, Anne in 4.1). Richard's soliloquy is the play's formal admission that Richard has one. The cost is the coherence of the I that had spoken so fluidly for four acts. The ghosts have not only indicted him; they have dispersed him across the pronouns he used to control.
sources: Harold Bloom, Shakespeare: The Invention of the Human (1998)
Maynard Mack, "The Jacobean Shakespeare: Some Observations on the Construction of the Tragedies" (1960)
Katharine Eisaman Maus, Inwardness and Theater in the English Renaissance (1995)

annotations.title: "A thousand several tongues" — interiority as courtroom
annotations.body: The soliloquy's formal peak is "My conscience hath a thousand several tongues, / And every tongue brings in a several tale, / And every tale condemns me for a villain." The three "several" (= separate) tongues are the exact form of the ghost procession: distinct voices, distinct tales, one verdict. Shakespeare converts the stage convention of serial haunting into the pattern of conscience itself. What is remarkable is that the speech's eventual conclusion — "There is no creature loves me; / And if I die, no soul shall pity me: / Nay, wherefore should they, since that I myself / Find in myself no pity to myself?" — is not self-pity but accurate self-diagnosis. Richard has understood. The play's morality does not require him to repent; it requires him to see. He sees, and it changes nothing about his choice in the coming battle. This is perhaps the play's darkest theological move.
sources: Katharine Eisaman Maus, Inwardness and Theater in the English Renaissance (1995)
A. C. Bradley, Shakespearean Tragedy (1904)
Harold Bloom, Shakespeare: The Invention of the Human (1998)

annotations.title: "I shall despair" — the specific theological weight
annotations.body: Richard's "I shall despair" is the precise theological terminus the ghosts have been aiming at. Within Christian tradition, despair (Latin desperatio) is the sin against the Holy Spirit — the refusal to believe that one's sins are forgivable, which therefore precludes repentance and guarantees damnation. Unlike pride, envy, or murder, despair cannot itself be forgiven because it refuses the possibility of forgiveness. This is why every ghost has used the exact phrase "despair, and die!" The ghosts are not wishing Richard physical death; they are wishing him a specific kind of death — unrepentant. Richard's admission "I shall despair" signals that he has accepted the curse's theological operation. He cannot repent now. The play has placed him in the one condition medieval-Christian doctrine identified as beyond rescue. Whether Shakespeare endorsed this theology or dramatized it because his audience held it is scholarly debate; what the play demonstrates is that its moral world requires the doctrine to work.
sources: Susan Snyder, "The Left Hand of God: Despair in Medieval and Renaissance Tradition" (1965)
Debora K. Shuger, Habits of Thought in the English Renaissance (1990)
Richard Strier, The Unrepentant Renaissance (2011)

annotations.title: "Conscience is but a word that cowards use"
annotations.body: Richard's oration to his soldiers includes the couplet "Conscience is but a word that cowards use, / Devised at first to keep the strong in awe: / Our strong arms be our conscience, swords our law." The lines are Richard's most explicitly nihilist public speech. They also stand in direct contradiction to the soliloquy he delivered privately moments earlier ("A thousand several tongues"). Shakespeare gives us Richard's public self and private self within a few hundred lines and lets the gap speak. He is lying to his troops and knows he is lying. The "Jockey of Norfolk" placard — "Jockey of Norfolk, be not too bold, / For Dickon thy master is bought and sold" — mounted on Norfolk's tent and dismissed as "A thing devised by the enemy," continues the motif. Richard is surrounded by warnings he is now refusing to hear after having heard them acutely just hours before. The tragedy of the final act is that Richard has seen clearly and chosen anyway.
sources: Stephen Greenblatt, Tyrant: Shakespeare on Politics (2018)
A. P. Rossiter, Angel with Horns (1961)
Wolfgang Clemen, A Commentary on Shakespeare's Richard III (1968)

annotations.title: Richmond's prayer mirrors Richard's soliloquy
annotations.body: Shakespeare stages the two commanders' nights in tight structural parallel. Each is visited by the same ghosts; each delivers a pre-battle speech. Richmond's prayer before sleep (105–113) — "O Thou, whose captain I account myself, / Look on my forces with a gracious eye" — asks God to "make us thy ministers of chastisement." His post-ghost waking is the mirror-opposite of Richard's: "my soul is very jocund / In the remembrance of so fair a dream." Same ghosts, opposite effects. Richmond's public oration to his troops (225–272) casts the battle as defence of "God's enemies" against "foreign mercenaries," framing the Tudor arrival as national restoration. The parallel structure is doing particular theological work: the same supernatural visitation blesses one and curses the other, because the providential order recognizes the two men differently. Whether the audience must endorse this claim is another matter — but the scene asks us to see its shape.
sources: E. M. W. Tillyard, Shakespeare's History Plays (1944)
Phyllis Rackin, Stages of History (1990)
Wolfgang Clemen, A Commentary on Shakespeare's Richard III (1968)

annotations.title: The ghosts as Shakespeare's laboratory for later tragedies
annotations.body: This scene is where Shakespeare first stages the essential pattern of tragic interiority that he will refine in Macbeth's banquet scene (Banquo's ghost) and Hamlet's encounters with his father. The technique — the sinner's recognition as a procession of specific indictments rather than a single voice of doom — is here perfected. Richard's "thousand several tongues" will reappear in Macbeth's "the multitudinous seas incarnadine" and Lady Macbeth's "all the perfumes of Arabia." The idea that guilt is serial, specific, and cumulative, and that recognition is not rescue, is the doctrine of the mature tragedies working itself out here in early form. What is different in Richard III is that Shakespeare allows the ghosts to be literal — they arrive, they speak, they exit — whereas in the later tragedies the ghosts will become (or threaten to become) hallucinatory. Here Shakespeare uses medieval stage convention without apology; in the later plays he will interrogate the convention itself.
sources: A. C. Bradley, Shakespearean Tragedy (1904)
Marjorie Garber, Shakespeare's Ghost Writers (1987)
Stephen Greenblatt, Hamlet in Purgatory (2001)

trials.prompt: How is the ghost procession in 5.3 formally organized?
options: Each ghost speaks only once, with no structural pattern
Each ghost curses Richard ("despair, and die!") and then turns to bless the sleeping Richmond ("live, and flourish!") — a double address in strict symmetry
The ghosts speak together as a chorus in unison
The ghosts speak only to Richmond, not to Richard

trials.prompt: What is the dramatic function of the ghost procession?
options: To provide visual spectacle before the battle
To serve as a moral ledger — each ghost naming a specific murder, together recited in chronological order, enacting conscience as a courtroom of specific testimonies
To confirm that Richard will die the next day
To give Richmond a prophetic dream of victory

trials.prompt: Richard's soliloquy begins "Richard loves Richard; that is, I am I." What does the speech dramatize?
options: Richard's settled self-knowledge
Self-division: a self that cannot hold together — the pronouns unravel, he can neither condemn nor absolve himself in consistent voice, the I of four acts disperses under the ghosts' indictments
Richard's new resolve to rule better
A religious conversion

trials.prompt: "My conscience hath a thousand several tongues" (190–192) does what formally?
options: Restates a proverb from Ecclesiastes
Converts the stage convention of serial haunting (the ghost procession) into the pattern of conscience itself — distinct voices, distinct tales, one verdict
Predicts the number of soldiers in Richmond's army
Refers to the number of Senators in Rome

trials.prompt: Why is Richard's "I shall despair" theologically significant?
options: It is idiomatic, meaning only temporary discouragement

In Christian tradition, despair (desperatio) is the sin against the Holy Spirit — refusal of forgiveness, which precludes repentance and guarantees damnation. Every ghost's "despair, and die!" aims at this specific theological terminus
It marks Richard's conversion to Calvinism
It is a quotation from Martin Luther

trials.prompt: Richard's public oration — "Conscience is but a word that cowards use" — contradicts what?
options: A specific statute in Edwardian law
His own private soliloquy moments earlier ("A thousand several tongues") — Shakespeare gives us Richard's public and private selves within a few hundred lines and lets the gap speak
Richmond's rallying speech
An earlier promise to his mother

trials.prompt: Richmond's post-ghost waking (225–228) stands in what relation to Richard's?
options: The same: both are terrified
Mirror-opposite: same ghosts, opposite effects — Richmond's soul is "very jocund" while Richard despairs, staging the providential order's differential recognition of the two men
Unrelated, occurring in separate acts
Briefer and less developed

trials.prompt: The ghost scene is most important as Shakespeare's early laboratory for which of his later plays' techniques?
options: The comic subplots of Love's Labour's Lost
The inward recognition structures of Macbeth and Hamlet — guilt as serial, specific, and cumulative, and recognition as not rescue
The pastoral conventions of As You Like It
The historical method of the Henry IV plays

trials.prompt: When Richard says "Is there a murderer here? No. Yes, I am: / Then fly. What, from myself?" the syntactic feature most indicative of his breakdown is:
options: The use of short questions — rhetorical convention only
The pronouns unravelling — "myself, I myself, I, I" — showing a self that can no longer sustain the coherent I of earlier acts
The shift from iambic pentameter to prose
The absence of biblical quotation

trials.prompt: What is the paper Norfolk finds on his tent in the morning (263–265)?
options: A royal commission from Richard
A jeering couplet: "Jockey of Norfolk, be not too bold, / For Dickon thy master is bought and sold" — warning Norfolk that Richard's army is betrayed
A priest's absolution certificate
A Welsh battle-song
